personality_voice.book_baseline: Binns is defined by a single, deeply consistent personality: oblivious, droning, utterly detached from the living world. He speaks in a 'flat drone like an old vacuum cleaner,' uses a 'dry, wheezy voice,' and is 'serenely unaware' that students are not paying attention. He gets students' names wrong repeatedly (Miss Grant, O'Flaherty, Pennyfeather, Perkins) — not as a joke but as a genuine symptom of his disconnection. When Hermione forces him to discuss the Chamber of Secrets, he is visibly thrown, annoyed, and ultimately regretful: 'I regret telling you such a foolish story.' He is dismissive of myth and legend ('arrant nonsense,' 'ludicrous tale'), and his irritation when pressed is sharp and uncharacteristic of his usual stupor. His voice, rhythm, and behavioral pattern are highly specific and consistent across all seven books.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film corpus is entirely empty. Binns does not appear in any film scene provided. The films replaced him with a portrait for the Chamber of Secrets exposition (delivered by the Sorting Hat or omitted entirely), and History of Magic as a class is never depicted on screen.
personality_voice.difference: The character is entirely absent from the films. His voice, drone, obliviousness, name-mangling, irritability when interrupted, and dismissiveness of legend are all unrepresented.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: Complete absence means zero preservation of personality and voice. The score cannot be above the floor. A score of 4 (rather than 0) is not warranted — the character is functionally unrecognizable because he does not exist in the films at all. Score: 4, reflecting near-total absence with no observable film evidence.
personality_voice.evidence_status: Directly evidenced — the film corpus is empty; the book corpus is rich and consistent.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Binns serves two narrative functions. First, he is the structural embodiment of institutional tedium — his class is the canonical 'most boring,' and his existence as a ghost who didn't notice his own death is a recurring comic-atmospheric detail that textures Hogwarts as an institution. Second, and more concretely, he is the in-universe source of the Chamber of Secrets legend in CoS Book 2. He delivers the foundational exposition about Salazar Slytherin, the founding of Hogwarts, and the Chamber itself — information that is plot-critical. He also appears as a passive but recurring presence across Books 1, 3, 4, and 5, anchoring the rhythm of school life.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: Binns does not appear in the films. The Chamber of Secrets exposition is not delivered by him in the film. His institutional function as the emblem of academic tedium is entirely absent.
narrative_role_agency.difference: His plot-critical agency in CoS — delivering the Chamber legend — is transferred or omitted. His atmospheric-institutional function is entirely lost.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Both his narrative functions are eliminated. The transfer of the Chamber exposition away from Binns (or its omission) is a direct agency transfer per the Transfer Rule. Score: 3.
narrative_role_agency.evidence_status: Directly evidenced — film corpus is empty; book corpus confirms both functions.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Binns has no complex internal conflict. His 'motivation' is purely habitual: he teaches because he always has, unaware or indifferent to his own death. His dismissal of myth ('I deal with facts, not myths and legends') reflects a rigid empiricist identity. His brief irritation when pressed on the Chamber is the closest he comes to internal tension — he regrets indulging the class. These are shallow but consistent motivations.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: No film material exists to evaluate.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: Motivations cannot be assessed from absent material.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Complete absence. Even shallow motivations are unrepresented. Score: 2, reflecting that the character's defining motivational posture (habitual inertia, empiricist dismissiveness) is entirely absent from the films.
motivations_internal_conflict.evidence_status: Not enough evidence from film side; book baseline is directly evidenced.

character_arc.book_baseline: Binns has a deliberately flat arc — he does not change across seven books. This is intentional and thematically meaningful: he is the ghost who didn't notice he died, who continues unchanged. His flatness is the arc. The one moment of deviation — being 'completely thrown' by student interest in CoS — resolves immediately back to his default state.
character_arc.difference: A flat arc in the books should score full points if the films also present a flat arc. But the films present no arc at all because the character does not appear. Absence is not the same as a faithful flat arc — it is non-existence.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The scoring rule states a flat arc scores full points if flat in both books and films. However, the character does not appear in the films at all, so the flat arc is not preserved — it is simply absent. This is different from a faithful flat portrayal. A score of 8 reflects that the arc's flatness is not contradicted (no false arc is invented), but it cannot be credited as preserved when the character is absent.
character_arc.evidence_status: Directly evidenced in books; not enough evidence from films.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Binns's key relational dynamic is with his students collectively — specifically the dynamic of total mutual disengagement. Students sleep, doodle, copy Hermione's notes, and ignore him; he ignores them back, gets their names wrong, and is 'amazed to find the room in front of him full of people.' The one exception is his brief, uncomfortable engagement with Hermione in CoS, which reveals his dismissiveness of non-empirical knowledge and his discomfort with genuine student interest.
key_relationships.difference: The teacher-student dynamic, including the Hermione interaction, is entirely absent.
key_relationships.evidence_status: Directly evidenced in books; not enough evidence from films.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Binns's complexity is narrow but specific. The comedy of his obliviousness (floating above his chair, entering through the blackboard, not noticing his own death) is textural. His CoS scene is his richest moment: he is thrown by genuine interest, reluctantly engages, delivers plot-critical information with visible discomfort, snaps at students who push too far, and immediately regrets the digression. His name-mangling of students is a consistent, specific comic detail. Hermione's reference in DH to Binns having mentioned the Deathstick legends shows he is also a passive repository of obscure lore. These details collectively build a character who is more than a one-note bore.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: All of the above is lost: the CoS exposition scene, the name-mangling, the blackboard entrance, the floating above the chair, the DH lore reference, the Hermione dynamic.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The CoS scene alone — where Binns is reluctant, irritable, thrown, and ultimately regretful — is the character's most complex moment and is entirely absent. The atmospheric texture across five books is also lost. Score: 3.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.evidence_status: Directly evidenced in books; not enough evidence from films.

lost_or_transferred_material: Chamber of Secrets exposition (Slytherin history, founding of Hogwarts, Chamber legend) — delivered by Binns in the book, absent or transferred in the film
Binns's dismissal of myth and legend as a character-defining empiricist stance
Binns's visible discomfort and irritation when students show genuine interest (CoS Chapter 9)
Binns's immediate regret at having told the Chamber story ('I regret telling you such a foolish story')
Consistent name-mangling of students (Miss Grant, O'Flaherty, Pennyfeather, Perkins)
Physical comedy of entering through the blackboard and floating above his chair
Atmospheric function as institutional emblem of Hogwarts tedium across all seven books
Passive lore repository function (DH reference to Deathstick legends)
The teacher-student dynamic of mutual total disengagement
Hermione's unique ability to engage Binns — the only student to do so
score_caps_applied: Cap 4 (score above 70 not permitted when films preserve only broad identity but remove most defining characterization) — not applicable as score is already far below 70
Cap 3 (score above 75 not permitted when a core book function is transferred to another character) — Chamber of Secrets exposition transfer applies; score already below threshold
comparative.key_observations: Cuthbert Binns does not appear in any film scene in the provided corpus, and based on knowledge of the films, he was entirely cut from the adaptations — History of Magic as a class is never shown on screen. This is a case of complete omission rather than adaptation compression. His most significant narrative contribution — delivering the Chamber of Secrets legend in CoS — was either transferred or omitted. His atmospheric function across five books, his specific comic texture (name-mangling, blackboard entrance, obliviousness to his own death), and his one moment of genuine complexity (the CoS classroom scene) are all absent. The score of 21 reflects near-total non-preservation. Confidence is Medium rather than High because the film corpus is empty, meaning the evaluation rests on confirmed absence rather than on analysis of divergent film material — the conclusion is certain, but the one-sided evidence limits analytical depth.
